name: CAN-SPAM Act
summary: US federal law (15 USC §7701 et seq.) setting standards for commercial email: truthful sender identification, accurate subject lines, clear unsubscribe mechanism, honoring opt-outs within 10 business days.

evidence.claim: Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography and Marketing Act of 2003 (CAN-SPAM), codified at 15 USC §§7701-7713 and implemented by 16 CFR Part 316.

FTC CAN-SPAM Compliance Guide for Business detailing the seven main requirements: accurate header, truthful subject, identify as ad, valid postal address, unsubscribe link, honor opt-outs in 10 business days, monitor third-party senders.

FTC civil penalties for CAN-SPAM violations adjusted annually for inflation; 2024 maximum $51,744 per email (16 CFR 1.98). Multiple aggravating factors (deceptive subject lines, harvested addresses) can multiply penalties.

appliesTo: Senders of commercial email to US recipients
Affiliates and third parties sending on behalf of primary businesses
businessScope.note: Applies only to commercial email; transactional-only messages (receipts, account updates) have different disclosure requirements. Does NOT require opt-in consent (unlike GDPR/CASL).

body.officialName: Controlling the Assault of Non-Solicited Pornography and Marketing Act of 2003
body.scopeSummary: CAN-SPAM imposes seven requirements on commercial email: (1) non-deceptive From/To/Reply-To headers, (2) non-deceptive subject lines, (3) identification as an advertisement, (4) physical postal address of sender, (5) unsubscribe mechanism that works, (6) opt-out honored within 10 business days and permanent, (7) business is accountable even when hiring third parties to send email. Preempts most state anti-spam laws but allows state deceptive-practice laws and common-law fraud claims. Does NOT create a private right of action — only FTC, state AGs, and ISPs may enforce.
body.userRightsPlainLanguage: Every commercial email you receive must: tell you honestly who sent it, have a truthful subject line, identify itself as advertising, include a real physical address, and give you a working unsubscribe link. Once you click unsubscribe, they must stop emailing you within 10 business days — and can't transfer your address to another affiliate to send you the same content. You can't sue spammers yourself under CAN-SPAM, but you can report them to the FTC and your state AG, and forward the email to spam@uce.gov.

enforcementSignals.action: FTC CAN-SPAM enforcement action list with consent decrees and settlements
enforcementSignals.description: Regular cadence of CAN-SPAM enforcement; recent cases focus on affiliate-marketing schemes with deceptive subject lines.

enforcementSignals.action: FBI forward mailbox for CAN-SPAM complaints
